analogy: Think of "Sectors of the Indian Economy" through the chapter's own opening images: NOTES FOR THE TEACHER CHAPTER 2: SECTORS OF THE INDIAN ECONOMY An economy is best understood when we study its components or sectors. Sectoral classification can be done on the basis of several criteria. In this chapter, three types of classifications are discussed: primary/secondary/tertiary; organised/... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: NOTES FOR THE TEACHER, CHAPTER 2: SECTORS OF THE INDIAN ECONOMY, SECTORS, OF THE INDIAN ECONOMY. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Sectors of the Indian Economy is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that nOTES FOR THE TEACHER CHAPTER 2: SECTORS OF THE INDIAN ECONOMY An economy is best understood when we study its components or sectors.

The text explains that sectoral classification can be done on the basis of several criteria.

The text explains that in this chapter, three types of classifications are discussed: primary/secondary/tertiary; organised/ unorganised; and public/private.

The text explains that you can create a discussion about these types by taking examples familiar to the students and relate them to their daily life.

The text explains that it is important to emphasise the changing roles of sectors.

The text explains that this can be highlighted further by drawing attention of the students to the rapid growth of service sector.

The text explains that while elaborating the ideas provided in the chapter, the students may need to be familiarised with a few fundamental concepts such as Gross Domestic Product, Employment etc.

The text explains that since the students may find this difficult to understand, it is necessary to explain to them through examples.

The text explains that several activities and exercises are suggested in the chapter to help the students understand how a person’s activity could be placed — whether in the primary, secondary or tertiary, organised or unorganised, and public or private sector.

The text explains that you may encourage the students to talk to various working people around them (such as shop owners, casual workers, vegetable vendors, workshop mechanics, domestic workers etc.) to know more about how they live and work.

The text explains that based on such information, the students can be encouraged to develop their own classification of economic activities.

The text explains that another important issue to be highlighted is about the problems caused by the changes in the roles of sectors.

The text explains that the chapter has taken the example of unemployment and what the government can do to solve it.

The text explains that the declining importance of agriculture and growing importance of industry and services should be related to the experience of the children by taking more examples that they may observe in their day-to-day life.

The text explains that information derived from the media could be used for this purpose.

The text explains that you may encourage the students to bring important cuttings and stories from newspapers, which could be prominently displayed in storyboards, and encourage the class to discuss these issues.

The text explains that sources for Information The GVA data used in this chapter pertaining to Real Gross Value Added at Basic Prices by Industry of Origin at 2011–12 prices is taken from Economic Survey.

The text explains that it is a valuable source of GVA and other information relating to the Indian economy.

The text explains that due to change in methodology, latest data is not used in the chapter.

The text explains that nSO is an organisation under the Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation, Government of India.

The text explains that employment data is also available from other sources such as Census of India.

The text explains that sECTORS OF THE INDIAN ECONOMY CHAPTER 2 SECTORS OF ECONOMIC ACTIVITIES Let us look at these pictures.

The text explains that u U U U UNDERST NDERST NDERST NDERST NDERSTANDING ANDING ANDING ANDING ANDING E E E E ECONOMIC CONOMIC CONOMIC CONOMIC CONOMIC D D D D DEVEL EVEL EVEL EVEL EVELOPMENT OPMENT OPMENT OPMENT OPMENT 20 20 20 20 20 There are many activities that are undertaken by directly using natural resources.

The text explains that similarly, in the case of an activity like dairy, we are dependent on the biological process of the animals and availability of fodder etc.

The text explains that similarly, minerals and ores are also natural products.

The text explains that when we produce a good by exploiting natural resources, it is an activity of the primary sector.

The text explains that the product is not produced by nature but has to be made and therefore some process of manufacturing is essential.

The text explains that we convert earth into bricks and use bricks to make houses and buildings.

The text explains that these activities, by themselves, do not produce a good but they are an aid or a support for the production process.

The text explains that service sector also includes some essential services that may not directly help in the production of goods.

The text explains that s S S S SECTORS ECTORS ECTORS ECTORS ECTORS OF OF OF OF OF THE THE THE THE THE IIIIINDIAN NDIAN NDIAN NDIAN NDIAN E E E E ECONOMY CONOMY CONOMY CONOMY CONOMY 21 21 21 21 21 EXAMPLE Imagine what would happen if farmers refuse to sell sugarcane to a particular sugar mill.

The text explains that explain the difference between primary, secondary and tertiary sectors using examples other than those mentioned in the text.

The text explains that in an economy there could be one or more sectors which are dominant in terms of total production and employment, while other sectors are relatively small in size.

The text explains that s S S S SECTORS ECTORS ECTORS ECTORS ECTORS OF OF OF OF OF THE THE THE THE THE IIIIINDIAN NDIAN NDIAN NDIAN NDIAN E E E E ECONOMY CONOMY CONOMY CONOMY CONOMY 23 23 23 23 23 The value of final goods and services The value of final goods and services The value of final goods and services The value of final goods and services The value of final goods and services produced in each sector during a produced in each sector during a produced in each sector during a produced in each sector during a produced in each sector during a particular year provides the total particular year provides the total particular year provides the total particular year provides the total particular year provides the total production of the sector for that year. production of the sector for that year. production of the sector for that year. production of the sector for that year. production of the sector for that year.

The text explains that in India, the mammoth task of measuring GDP is undertaken by a central government ministry.

The text explains that this Ministry, with the help of various government departments of all the Indian states and union territories, collects information relating to total volume of goods and services and their prices and then estimates the GDP.

The text explains that recently Indian Government began to bring out the contribution of three sectors towards Gross Value Added (GVA) in place of the contribution of three sectors towards Gross Domestic Product to be at par with global practices.

The text explains that the GVA measures the contribution of three sectors of an economy after adjusting for taxes and subsidies.

The text explains that they were forced to do so as you read in history chapters.

The text explains that what does the history of developed countries indicate about the shifts that have taken place between sectors?

The text explains that in a developing country the government has to take responsibility for the provision of these services.

The text explains that s S S S SECTORS ECTORS ECTORS ECTORS ECTORS OF OF OF OF OF THE THE THE THE THE IIIIINDIAN NDIAN NDIAN NDIAN NDIAN E E E E ECONOMY CONOMY CONOMY CONOMY CONOMY 25 25 25 25 25 transport, trade, storage and the like, as we have already seen.

The text explains that in Chapter 4, we shall see examples of these new services and the reasons for their expansion.

The text explains that now you can directly see the changing importance of the sectors over the forty years.

The text explains that s S S S SECTORS ECTORS ECTORS ECTORS ECTORS OF OF OF OF OF THE THE THE THE THE IIIIINDIAN NDIAN NDIAN NDIAN NDIAN E E E E ECONOMY CONOMY CONOMY CONOMY CONOMY 27 27 27 27 27 How to Create More Employment?

The text explains that the government can spend some money or banks can provide a loan, to construct a well for her family to irrigate the land.

The text explains that give a few examples of public sector activities and explain why the government has taken them up.

The text explains that explain how public sector contributes to the economic development of a nation.

The text explains that the workers in the unorganised sector need protection on the following issues : wages, safety and health.

The text explains that a study in Ahmedabad found that out of 15,00,000 workers in the city, 11,00,000 worked in the unorganised sector.

The text explains that the total income of the city in this year (1997-1998) was Rs 60,000 million.

The text explains that out of this Rs 32,000 million was generated in the organised sector.

Source: NCERT Class 10 Understanding Economic Development, Chapter 2: Sectors of the Indian Economy

prelims_traps: Do not treat NOTES FOR THE TEACHER and CHAPTER 2: SECTORS OF THE INDIAN ECONOMY as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Sectors of the Indian Economy; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Sectors of the Indian Economy through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use NOTES FOR THE TEACHER and CHAPTER 2: SECTORS OF THE INDIAN ECONOMY to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

You can create a discussion about these types by taking examples familiar to the students and relate them to their daily life.
